title.ja: なんて恐ろしく賢いのでしょう！

topic.en: Asking questions, travel information, and being clever
topic.ja: 質問、旅行案内、そして賢く考えること
topic.fr: Poser des questions, voyager et être malin
topic.ko: 질문하기, 여행 정보, 그리고 영리하게 생각하기

core_reading_html.en: Tom wanted to visit his family in another city. At the station, he asked for a ticket and checked the train time. He stood on the correct platform and waited. During the trip, he asked many questions. He wanted good information. Later, he stayed in a small hotel. At night, he heard a strange noise. He felt afraid, but he listened carefully and made a clever choice. In the end, there was no danger. The police helped him, and everything was fine.
core_reading_html.ja: トムは家族に会うため別の町へ行きました。駅で きっぷ を買い、でんしゃ の時間を調べました。正しい ホーム で待ちながら、たくさんの しつもん をしました。役立つ じょうほう が欲しかったのです。その後、小さな ホテル に泊まりました。夜になると不思議な おと が聞こえました。少し こわい と感じましたが、よく聞いて かしこい 判断をしました。最後には危険はなく、けいさつ が助けてくれました。
core_reading_html.fr: Tom voulait rendre visite à sa famille dans une autre ville. À la gare, il a acheté un billet et vérifié l'heure du train. Il a attendu sur le bon quai. Pendant le voyage, il a posé beaucoup de questions. Il voulait de bonnes informations. Plus tard, il est resté dans un petit hôtel. La nuit, il a entendu un bruit étrange. Il avait peur, mais il a réfléchi et a fait un choix malin. Finalement, il n'y avait aucun danger. La police l'a aidé et tout allait bien.
core_reading_html.ko: 톰은 다른 도시에 있는 가족을 만나러 가고 싶었습니다. 역에서 표 를 사고 기차 시간을 확인했습니다. 그는 올바른 플랫폼 에서 기다렸습니다. 여행 중에 많은 질문 을 했습니다. 좋은 정보 를 얻고 싶었기 때문입니다. 나중에 작은 호텔 에 머물렀습니다. 밤에 이상한 소리 를 들었습니다. 그는 무서운 기분이 들었지만 차분히 생각하고 영리한 선택을 했습니다. 결국 위험은 없었고, 경찰 이 도와주어 모든 일이 잘 해결되었습니다.

en.definition: The place where people wait for trains.
Something you ask to get information.

ja.definition: でんしゃにのるためのけん。
レールのうえをはしるのりもの。
でんしゃをまつばしょ。
りょこうでとまるところ。

fr.definition: Endroit où l'on attend le train.
Lieu où l'on passe la nuit.

script.speaker: A
script.text: Good morning. Can you tell me the times of trains from Bristol back to Oxford, please?

script.speaker: B
script.text: Afternoon, evening? When do you want to come back?

script.speaker: B
script.text: About five o’clock. Right. Let’s have a look. There’s a train that leaves at 5.28, then there isn’t another one until 6.50.

script.speaker: A
script.text: And what time do they get in?

script.speaker: B
script.text: The 5.28 gets into Oxford at 6.54 and the 6.50 gets in at 8.10.

script.speaker: A
script.text: Hello. A return to Bristol, please.

script.speaker: C
script.text: How do you want to pay?

script.speaker: C
script.text: Here’s your change and your ticket.

script.speaker: A
script.text: Thank you. Which platform is it?

script.speaker: A
script.text: Why do you want to go?

script.speaker: B
script.text: At a hotel near the station.

script.speaker: A
script.text: Where did you go at the weekend?

script.speaker: Narrator
script.text: It was about 2 o’clock in the morning, and suddenly I woke up. I heard a noise. I got out of bed and went slowly downstairs. There was a light on in the living room. I listened carefully. I could hear two men speaking very quietly: ‘Burglars!’ I thought. ‘Two burglars!’ Immediately I ran back upstairs and phoned the police. I was really frightened. Fortunately the police arrived quickly. They opened the front door and went into the living room. Then they came upstairs to find me. ‘It’s all right now, sir,’ they explained. ‘We turned the television off for you!’

ja.title: なんて恐ろしく賢いのでしょう！
ja.label: 会話 1: 案内所で

script.speaker: A
script.text: おはようございます。ブリストルからオックスフォードまでの電車の時間を教えていただけますか？

script.speaker: B
script.text: 午後ですか、夕方ですか？ どのくらいの時間に戻りたいですか？

script.speaker: A
script.text: 今日の午後5時ごろです。

script.speaker: B
script.text: 5時ごろですね。ええと… 5時28分の電車があります。その次は6時50分までありませんね。

script.speaker: A
script.text: 到着時間は何時ですか？

script.speaker: B
script.text: 5時28分発はオックスフォードに6時54分着、6時50分発は8時10分着です。

script.speaker: A
script.text: どうもありがとうございました。

ja.title: なんて恐ろしく賢いのでしょう！
ja.label: 会話 2: 切符売り場で

script.speaker: A
script.text: こんにちは。ブリストルまでの往復切符をください。

script.speaker: C
script.text: 日帰り往復ですか、期間限定往復ですか？

script.speaker: A
script.text: 日帰りでお願いします。

script.speaker: C
script.text: お支払いはどうなさいますか？

script.speaker: A
script.text: ありがとうございます。何番線ですか？

script.speaker: A
script.text: わかりました。どうもありがとう。さようなら。

ja.title: なんて恐ろしく賢いのでしょう！
ja.label: 会話 3: 質問と答え

script.speaker: A
script.text: なぜ行きたいのですか？

script.speaker: A
script.text: 彼はどこに泊まっているのですか？

script.speaker: B
script.text: 駅の近くのホテルです。

script.speaker: A
script.text: なぜ彼らは来なかったのですか？

script.speaker: B
script.text: バスに乗り遅れたからです。

script.speaker: A
script.text: 彼はギターを弾きますか？

script.speaker: A
script.text: 週末はどこへ行きましたか？

ja.title: なんて恐ろしく賢いのでしょう！
ja.label: リスニング 1: 夜中の物音

script.speaker: ナレーター
script.text: 夜中の2時ごろ、突然目が覚めました。物音がしたのです。ベッドから出て、ゆっくりと階段を下りました。居間に明かりがついていました。よく耳を澄ますと、二人の男がとても静かに話しているのが聞こえました。『泥棒だ！』と思いました。『二人の泥棒だ！』すぐに二階に駆け上がり、警察に電話しました。本当に怖かったです。幸い警察はすぐに到着しました。彼らは玄関を開けて居間に入りました。それから私を探して二階に上がってきました。『もう大丈夫です、ご主人。テレビを消しておきましたので』と説明してくれました。

script.speaker: A
script.text: Bonjour. Pouvez-vous me donner les horaires des trains de Bristol retour à Oxford, s'il vous plaît ?

script.speaker: B
script.text: Après-midi ou soir ? À quelle heure voulez-vous revenir ?

script.speaker: B
script.text: Vers cinq heures. D'accord. Voyons voir. Il y a un train à 17h28, et le suivant n'est qu'à 18h50.

script.speaker: B
script.text: Celui de 17h28 arrive à Oxford à 18h54, et celui de 18h50 arrive à 20h10.

script.speaker: A
script.text: Bonjour. Un aller-retour pour Bristol, s'il vous plaît.

script.speaker: C
script.text: Aller-retour journée ou aller-retour longue durée ?

script.speaker: A
script.text: Voici un billet de vingt livres.

script.speaker: C
script.text: Voici votre monnaie et votre billet.

script.speaker: B
script.text: Pour rendre visite à ma famille.

script.speaker: B
script.text: Dans un hôtel près de la gare.

script.speaker: A
script.text: Est-ce qu'il joue de la guitare ?

fr.title: Comme c'est terriblement intelligent !
fr.label: Écoute 1 : Des bruits dans la nuit

script.speaker: Narrateur
script.text: Il était environ deux heures du matin, et soudain je me suis réveillé. J'ai entendu un bruit. Je suis sorti du lit et je suis descendu lentement. Il y avait de la lumière dans le salon. J'ai écouté attentivement. J'entendais deux hommes parler tout doucement : « Des cambrioleurs ! » ai-je pensé. « Deux cambrioleurs ! » J'ai tout de suite remonté à l'étage et j'ai appelé la police. J'étais vraiment effrayé. Heureusement, la police est arrivée rapidement. Ils ont ouvert la porte d'entrée et sont entrés dans le salon. Puis ils sont montés à l'étage pour me trouver. « Tout va bien maintenant, monsieur, » ont-ils expliqué. « Nous avons éteint la télévision pour vous ! »

script.speaker: A
script.text: 안녕하세요. 브리스틀에서 옥스퍼드로 돌아가는 기차 시간을 알려 주시겠어요?

script.speaker: B
script.text: 5시쯤이군요. 자, 한번 볼게요. 5시 28분 기차가 있고, 그다음은 6시 50분까지 없네요.

script.speaker: A
script.text: 안녕하세요. 브리스틀까지 왕복표 한 장 주세요.

script.speaker: 내레이터
script.text: 새벽 2시쯤이었어요. 갑자기 잠에서 깼는데, 소음이 들렸어요. 침대에서 나와 천천히 계단을 내려갔죠. 거실에 불이 켜져 있었어요. 조용히 귀를 기울였더니 두 남자가 아주 조용히 말하는 소리가 났어요. ‘도둑이구나!’라고 생각했죠. ‘두 명의 도둑!’ 곧바로 다시 위층으로 뛰어올라가 경찰에 전화했어요. 정말 무서웠거든요. 다행히 경찰이 빨리 도착했어요. 경찰관들이 현관문을 열고 거실로 들어갔어요. 그런 다음 저를 찾으러 위층으로 올라왔죠. ‘이제 괜찮습니다, 손님. 텔레비전을 꺼 드렸습니다.’라고 설명하더라고요.

extra_reading_html.en: After hearing the young man's story about Bertha, the children on the train were excited. 'That's a terrible story!' said the aunt. 'No it isn't!' shouted the children. 'It's the best story ever!' The young man smiled. 'Ah, the train's stopping. It's my station.' He got off the train, leaving the aunt looking annoyed and the children looking interested. What do you think is the moral of Bertha's story? Is it 'It pays to be good', 'It never pays to be good', or 'It doesn't always pay to be good'? The story suggests that being extremely good doesn't always protect you from danger. Sometimes, it's better to be clever than horribly good.
extra_reading_html.ja: ベルタについての青年のお話を聞いた後、列車に乗っていた子供たちは興奮していました。「なんてひどいお話なの！」と叔母は言いました。「そんなことないよ！」子供たちは叫びました。「今までで一番最高のお話だよ！」青年は微笑みました。「ああ、列車が止まる。私の駅だ。」彼は列車を降りました。叔母は苛立った様子で、子供たちは興味深そうな様子でした。ベルタのお話の教訓は何だと思いますか？「正直者は報われる」「正直者は損をする」、それとも「正直者でいることが必ずしも報われるとは限らない」でしょうか。このお話は、極端に「良い子」でいることが、常に危険から守ってくれるわけではないことを示唆しています。時には、ひどく良い子でいるよりも、賢くある方が良いこともあるのです。
extra_reading_html.fr: Après avoir écouté l'histoire du jeune homme sur Bertha, les enfants dans le train étaient enthousiastes. « C'est une histoire terrible ! » a dit la tante. « Non, ça ne l'est pas ! » ont crié les enfants. « C'est la meilleure histoire de tous les temps ! » Le jeune homme a souri. « Ah, le train s'arrête. C'est ma gare. » Il est descendu du train, laissant la tante avec un air agacé et les enfants avec un air intéressé. Quelle est, selon vous, la morale de l'histoire de Bertha ? Est-ce « Il est payant d'être bon », « Il n'est jamais payant d'être bon » ou « Il n'est pas toujours payant d'être bon » ? L'histoire suggère qu'être extrêmement bon ne vous protège pas toujours du danger. Parfois, il vaut mieux être malin qu'horriblement bon.
extra_reading_html.ko: 벨타에 관한 청년의 이야기를 들은 후, 기차에 타고 있던 아이들은 신이 났습니다. "정말 끔찍한 이야기구나!" 숙모가 말했습니다. "아니에요!" 아이들이 소리쳤습니다. "지금까지 들은 이야기 중 최고예요!" 청년은 미소를 지었습니다. "아, 기차가 멈추네요. 제가 내릴 곳입니다." 그는 기차에서 내렸고, 숙모는 짜증이 난 표정으로, 아이들은 흥미로운 표정으로 남았습니다. 벨타 이야기의 교훈은 무엇이라고 생각하나요? '착하게 사는 것은 보람이 있다', '착하게 사는 것은 결코 보람이 없다', 아니면 '착하게 사는 것이 항상 보람 있는 것은 아니다'일까요? 이 이야기는 지나치게 착하게 행동하는 것이 항상 위험으로부터 당신을 보호해주지는 않는다는 점을 시사합니다. 때로는 지독하게 착한 것보다 영리하게 행동하는 것이 더 나을 때도 있습니다.

en.question: The young man's story about Bertha made the children feel bored.
en.explanation: False. The children thought it was 'the best story ever' and were excited.

en.question: Bertha won three medals for her good behavior.
en.explanation: True. She won medals for being punctual, polite, and the best child.

en.question: The aunt was happy with the young man's story.
en.explanation: False. The aunt called it a 'terrible story' and looked annoyed.

en.question: The moral of the story suggests that being good is always the safest option.
en.explanation: False. The story suggests that being extremely good doesn't always protect you from danger.

en.question: The young man got off the train at his destination.
en.explanation: True. He said 'It's my station' and got off.

ja.question: 青年のお話は子供たちを退屈させた。
ja.explanation: 誤りです。子供たちは「今までで最高のお話」だと興奮していました。

ja.question: ベルタは行儀の良さで3つのメダルを獲得した。
ja.explanation: 真実です。遅刻しない、礼儀正しいなどの理由でメダルを得ました。

ja.question: 叔母は青年のお話に満足していた。
ja.explanation: 誤りです。叔母は「ひどい話だ」と言い、苛立っていました。

ja.question: このお話の教訓は、善良でいることが常に最も安全だと言っている。
ja.explanation: 誤りです。常に善良であることが危険を防ぐとは限らないと示唆しています。

ja.question: 青年は自分の駅で列車を降りた。
ja.explanation: 真実です。自分の駅だと言って降りました。

fr.question: L'histoire du jeune homme a ennuyé les enfants.
fr.explanation: Faux. Les enfants étaient enthousiastes et ont adoré l'histoire.

fr.question: Bertha a gagné trois médailles pour sa bonne conduite.
fr.explanation: Vrai. Elle a été récompensée pour son comportement exemplaire.

fr.question: La tante a aimé l'histoire racontée par le jeune homme.
fr.explanation: Faux. La tante a trouvé l'histoire terrible et semblait agacée.

fr.question: La morale suggère qu'être bon est toujours la meilleure protection.
fr.explanation: Faux. L'histoire suggère qu'être trop bon ne protège pas toujours.

fr.question: Le jeune homme est descendu à sa gare.
fr.explanation: Vrai. Il a quitté le train une fois arrivé à sa destination.

ko.question: 청년의 이야기는 아이들을 지루하게 만들었다.
ko.explanation: 거짓입니다. 아이들은 그 이야기가 '최고'라며 매우 흥분했습니다.

ko.question: 벨타는 착한 행동으로 세 개의 메달을 받았다.
ko.explanation: 사실입니다. 정직하고 예의 바른 행동으로 메달을 땄습니다.

ko.question: 숙모는 청년이 들려준 이야기에 기뻐했다.
ko.explanation: 거짓입니다. 숙모는 이야기가 끔찍하다고 하며 짜증을 냈습니다.

ko.question: 이 이야기의 교훈은 착하게 사는 것이 항상 가장 안전하다고 말한다.
ko.explanation: 거짓입니다. 지나치게 착한 것이 항상 안전한 것은 아니라고 합니다.

ko.question: 청년은 자신의 역에서 기차를 내렸다.
ko.explanation: 사실입니다. 내릴 역에 도착해 기차에서 내렸습니다.

en.text: When you travel, it's helpful to ask questions and get good information.
If you hear a strange noise at night, try to make a clever choice.

ja.text: 旅行では 質問をする と 良い情報を得る ことが大切です。
夜に 変な音を聞く と、賢い選択をする のが大事です。

fr.text: Quand on voyage, c'est utile de poser des questions et obtenir de bonnes informations.
Si vous entendre un bruit étrange la nuit, essayez de faire un choix malin.

ko.text: 여행할 때는 질문을 하다고 좋은 정보를 얻다는 것이 중요해요.
밤에 이상한 소리를 듣다면 영리한 선택을 하다는 것이 필요해요.

grammar_html.en:
1. Question words

Question words are used to ask for specific information. Common question words: who, what, where, when, why, which, how, how many, how much.

  • • Where do you live? In London.
  • • When did the first man walk on the moon? 1969.
  • • How much does an African elephant weigh? 5–7 tonnes.

2. Adjectives vs Adverbs

Adjectives describe nouns. Adverbs describe verbs. Most adverbs are formed by adding -ly to the adjective.

  • • quick meal (adjective) → eat quickly (adverb)
  • • careful driver (adjective) → drive carefully (adverb)
  • • hard life (adjective) → work hard (adverb - irregular, same form)
  • • good cook (adjective) → cook well (adverb - irregular)

3. -ed / -ing adjectives

-ed adjectives describe feelings (how someone feels). -ing adjectives describe things or situations (what causes the feeling).

  • • The book was interesting. I was interested.
  • • The lesson was boring. The students were bored.

content_check_question.en: In this unit, you learned about question forms (who, what, where, when, why, which, how), adverbs vs adjectives, and describing feelings (bored, excited, worried, etc.). How do you form a question with a question word? What is the difference between an adjective and an adverb? What is the difference between 'bored' and 'boring'?

content_check_keywords.en: question words, adverbs, adjectives, feelings, -ed/-ing, train travel

content_check_placeholder.en: To make a question with a question word, you put the question word first, then the auxiliary verb, then the subject...

deepening_question.en: The story of Bertha shows a contrast between being 'horribly good' and being clever. Do you agree with the moral 'It doesn't always pay to be good'? Why or why not? Can you think of a situation where being too good might be a disadvantage?

deepening_placeholder.en: I agree/disagree because... For example, if you are too good...

critical_question.en: In this unit, you learned to ask different types of questions (with who, what, where, when, why, how) and to describe feelings using -ed and -ing adjectives. Why is it important to know how to ask different types of questions in English? How can using the correct adjective (e.g., 'I'm bored' vs 'The movie is boring') help you communicate more clearly when describing your feelings?

critical_placeholder.en: Knowing different question types is important because... Using the correct adjective helps because...
